nativeRange.habitat: Sandy and rubble areas of outer lagoons, seaward reefs, and estuaries, typically at depths of 2–43 m, generally found sharing a burrow with an alpheid (pistol) shrimp

decorPreferences: live rock with rubble at base

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied carnivorous diet of mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and finely chopped fresh or frozen marine seafood. Has been reported to do best with at least two small feedings per day. Sifts sand and picks small invertebrates from the burrow entrance in the wild.

avoidWith: other Amblyeleotris individuals unless introduced as a confirmed pair
large crustaceans that may compete for substrate

summary: The Steinitz Goby (Amblyeleotris steinitzi) is a small, peaceful Indo-Pacific shrimp-goby that generally forms a burrow-sharing partnership with a pistol shrimp. Considered beginner-friendly in a quiet reef tank with a deep sand bed and a tightly-sealed lid.
careNotes:
The Steinitz Goby is a member of the shrimp-goby group, a guild of small reef fish that live in close partnership with alpheid pistol shrimp. In the wild, this species almost always shares a burrow with a pistol shrimp — typically Alpheus djeddensis or a related species. The arrangement is mutually beneficial: the shrimp, which is nearly blind, excavates and maintains the burrow while keeping one antenna in contact with the goby at the entrance. The goby acts as lookout, and at the first sign of a threat it darts into the burrow — the shrimp, feeling that motion through its antenna, follows immediately. Pairing one of these gobies with a compatible pistol shrimp in captivity is generally optional, but the partnership tends to bring out the species' most natural and interesting behavior.

A deep sand bed — commonly advised at 5–8 cm or more — is generally considered essential, ideally with mixed rubble at the base of the rockwork so the pair has structure to dig under. Without suitable burrowing material, the goby is reported to be stressed and may remain unusually reclusive.

Like many small reef gobies, this species has been reported to jump from open-top aquariums, so a tightly sealed lid or mesh top is generally advised. It is otherwise considered hardy and fairly disease-resistant.

The Steinitz Goby is generally peaceful toward unrelated tankmates but has been known to show aggression toward conspecifics — it is best kept singly or as a confirmed pair introduced simultaneously. Larger gobies or other shrimp-goby species sharing the same tank may also draw territorial behavior.

Aquarium specimens are commonly reported in the 8–12 cm range at maturity.
breedingNotes: The Steinitz Goby has been reported to spawn in captivity, though successful reproduction is generally considered uncommon. Pairs typically lay eggs inside the shared burrow, and the male is reported to guard the clutch. Larval rearing has not been widely documented in the hobby and is generally considered the limiting step in achieving successful captive breeding.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs have been reported to spawn occasionally inside the shared burrow, with the male reported to guard the clutch. Larval rearing has not been widely documented in the hobby and is generally considered the limiting step.
